title: Room Database
objectives: Understand Room via a real-life analogy
Model entities, DAOs, and the database class
Use Flow-returning DAOs for reactive UI
Write migrations that don't lose user data

sections.markdown:
**Think of a paper filing cabinet at an office.**

- **The cabinet itself (Room `@Database`)** — the physical piece of furniture holding all the folders. It has a version number stamped on it ('Cabinet v3 — installed 2024-03').
- **The folders inside (Room `@Entity` classes)** — one folder per table: 'Customers', 'Orders', 'Products'. Each folder has a strict format: name, phone, address on Customer forms; item, price, date on Order forms.
- **The office clerks (Room `@Dao` interfaces)** — you don't dig through the cabinet yourself. You hand a request slip to a clerk: 'get me all customers who ordered last month'. The clerks know the drawer, the layout, and hand back exactly what you asked for. Each clerk knows only a few operations — no fumbling.
- **The old way (raw SQLite)** — you opening the cabinet yourself, remembering column names, writing SQL by hand, hoping you spelled 'customer' the same as when you set up the cabinet. Typos crash at runtime.
- **The Room way** — every SQL query the clerks accept is **checked at compile time**. Column name typo? Won't build. Query returns 3 columns but your Kotlin class has 4? Won't build. Type mismatch? Won't build.

That compile-time safety is Room's superpower: **the compiler is your test suite for SQL correctness.**

**Room** (part of Android Jetpack) is a SQLite abstraction library that gives you a compile-time-checked, coroutine-native ORM. You declare **Entities** (tables), **DAOs** (query methods), and a **Database** class. Room generates the SQLite plumbing; your SQL queries are verified at compile time. DAO methods can return coroutines, Flow, LiveData, or Paging sources.
## The three building blocks

rows: A Kotlin class that maps to a database table. Each field is a column.
`data class User(@PrimaryKey val id: Int, val name: String)`
An interface with query methods. Room generates the impl.
Abstract class listing entities and DAOs; the DB entry point.
`abstract class AppDb : RoomDatabase() { abstract fun userDao(): UserDao }`

sections.markdown: ## The full picture
## Why Room is a game-changer

rows: Typos in columns, wrong types, invalid SQL — all fail to compile. Bugs caught in your IDE.
Change the table anywhere → any observer of the Flow rerenders. UI stays in sync with data automatically.
Declare per-version migrations; Room verifies with schema JSONs.
Return `PagingSource<Int, User>` from a DAO — Paging 3 handles the rest.
In-memory DB (`inMemoryDatabaseBuilder`) makes DAO tests fast and hermetic.

sections.markdown: **The interview one-liner:** 'Room is SQLite with a compiler.' Everything else — Flow returns, migrations, transactions — flows from that core insight. The compiler validates your SQL against your Kotlin types the moment you save the file.
## Relationships — @Embedded, @Relation
## Migrations — the thing everyone gets wrong
## Common beginner mistakes
**Mistake 1: Calling a DAO method on the main thread.** Runs the SQL on the UI thread → ANR + IllegalStateException from Room. Fix: mark DAO methods `suspend` or return `Flow` — Room enforces it.
**Mistake 2: `.fallbackToDestructiveMigration()` in production.** This wipes user data whenever the schema version changes. Fine for prototypes. Catastrophic when you ship it.
**Mistake 3: Making Room `Room.databaseBuilder(...).build()` in every screen.** Building the database is expensive. Do it ONCE via Hilt / a singleton and inject the DAO.
**Mistake 4: Storing complex objects as Blobs.** Room stores primitives. For enums/dates/JSON use `@TypeConverter`. Don't `Serializable` your way through it — you'll regret it at migration time.

**Mistake 5: Not using transactions for multi-step writes.** Two `INSERT` statements without `@Transaction` can leave the DB in a half-updated state on crash. Wrap related mutations in `@Transaction`.
## Best practices
**1. Return `Flow<T>` from DAOs.** Any change anywhere in the app rerenders any observer — zero-effort reactivity.
**2. Provide a single Database instance via Hilt.** `@Provides @Singleton fun provideDb(app) = Room.databaseBuilder(app, AppDb::class.java, "app.db").build()`
**3. Write migrations for every version bump.** Never destructive-migrate real users.
**4. Test DAOs with an in-memory Room DB.** Fast, hermetic, no fixture files. `Room.inMemoryDatabaseBuilder(...)`.
**5. Enable `exportSchema = true` and commit schema JSON.** Room writes a JSON file per version; helps you diff schema changes in PRs and catch accidental breaks.
**6. Use `@Transaction` for reads that join multiple tables.** Guarantees a consistent snapshot.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — CRUD app.** User entity, DAO with insert/get/delete, click a button to add, list all users.

**Why:** Build muscle memory.

**Practice 2 (Medium) — Reactive list.** Convert the DAO to return `Flow<List<User>>`; a ViewModel exposes it; a Fragment renders. Add a user → list updates automatically.

**Why:** Feel the reactivity payoff.

**Practice 3 (Medium) — Relationships.** Add Order entity with a foreign key to User; write a query that returns UserWithOrders.

**Why:** Real apps have relationships.

**Practice 4 (Hard) — Migration.** Ship v1 with User(id, name). Ship v2 that adds `email`. Write MIGRATION_1_2 and verify user data survives.

**Why:** Migrations are a rite of passage.

**Practice 5 (Expert) — Paging 3 + Room.** DAO returns `PagingSource<Int, User>`; UI renders with PagingDataAdapter; source loads more as you scroll.

**Why:** Real production lists live here.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Room?**

**A:** Room is Android Jetpack's SQLite abstraction — a compile-time-checked, coroutine-native ORM. You declare `@Entity` classes (tables), `@Dao` interfaces (queries), and a `@Database` class (entry point). At compile time, Room parses every `@Query` string, verifies the columns and types match your Entity, and generates the underlying SQLite code. The result: SQL typos, wrong column types, and mismatched result mappings all fail to *compile*, not at runtime. DAOs can return `suspend fun`, `Flow<T>` (reactive!), `LiveData<T>`, or `PagingSource<Int, T>` — matching whatever pattern your UI uses. It's the successor to raw SQLite + manual `Cursor` code that dominated Android for years.

**Q2: What are the three main annotations?**

**A:** **`@Entity`** — marks a class as a table. `@PrimaryKey` on the ID field, `@ColumnInfo` to rename columns, `@Ignore` to skip a field, `@Embedded` to inline another class's fields. **`@Dao`** — marks an interface as a data access object. Method-level annotations: `@Query("SELECT ...")` for arbitrary SQL, `@Insert` / `@Update` / `@Delete` for convenience CRUD. Room implements the interface at compile time. **`@Database`** — marks an abstract class as the DB entry point. Lists `entities = [...]`, sets `version = N`, and declares `abstract fun someDao(): SomeDao`. `Room.databaseBuilder(...)` builds an instance. Together they cover 95% of production Room usage.

**Q3: How does Room integrate with Coroutines and Flow?**

**A:** Room DAO methods can return: (1) **`suspend fun`** — one-shot query on a background dispatcher; suspends caller until done. (2) **`Flow<T>`** — reactive: emits a new value every time any row in the query's referenced tables changes. Cold flow: work starts on first collection. (3) **`LiveData<T>`** — same reactive behavior, lifecycle-aware. (4) **`PagingSource<K, T>`** — for Paging 3 integration. **Flow is the killer feature** — a UI observing `Flow<List<User>>` automatically re-renders whenever anyone inserts/updates/deletes a user, anywhere in the app. No manual invalidation. Combined with `viewModelScope` for launch, `stateIn` for hot-conversion, and `collectAsState()` in Compose, Room + Flow give you effortless reactive persistence.

**Q5: Room vs raw SQLite vs realm vs SQLDelight?**

**A:** **Raw SQLite** — full control, no dependencies, but manual `Cursor` handling, runtime typos, tedious. Nobody chooses this today. **Room** — Google's official, compile-time SQL check, Flow-first, best Jetpack integration, coroutine-native. Default choice for Android. **Realm** — third-party object database; different query DSL; strong reactive story; but less-idiomatic Android integration and a bigger runtime. **SQLDelight** — JetBrains/Cash App, generates types from `.sq` SQL files (opposite of Room's Kotlin-first approach); popular in Kotlin Multiplatform because it targets both Android and iOS. **For pure Android** — pick Room. **For KMP with shared DB** — SQLDelight is a strong option.

**Q6: What's `@Transaction` and when is it required?**

**A:** `@Transaction` guarantees a group of DB operations runs atomically — either all succeed or none do. Two uses: (1) **Multi-step writes** — `@Transaction suspend fun replaceAll(users): { clearAll(); insertAll(users) }` — no half-updated state if a crash hits between the two DAO calls. (2) **Multi-table reads with relationships** — required when a method returns `@Relation` joined results (e.g., `UserWithOrders`), because Room actually runs multiple queries under the hood. `@Transaction` ensures the join sees a consistent snapshot. In general, wrap any related write operations in `@Transaction`. It's inexpensive and prevents whole classes of consistency bugs.

**Q7: How do you test Room DAOs?**

**A:** Use `Room.inMemoryDatabaseBuilder(context, AppDb::class.java).build()` — the DB lives in RAM, is torn down after each test, has zero I/O overhead. Combine with Coroutines' `runTest {}` to drive suspend DAO calls, and standard JUnit assertions. Fast, hermetic, deterministic — a good DAO test suite runs 100+ tests in under a second. **For migrations,** use `MigrationTestHelper` — creates the DB at an old version, runs your migration, verifies the new schema matches. For **integration tests** with a real SQLite file, use `Room.databaseBuilder(context, AppDb::class.java, "test.db")` inside an instrumented test. But the in-memory DB is the daily driver.

**Q8: What are `@Embedded` and `@Relation`?**

**A:** **`@Embedded`** — inline another class's fields directly into an entity's columns. Great for value objects: `@Embedded val address: Address` — Address's `street`, `city`, `zip` become columns of the parent table. No separate table. **`@Relation`** — signals a one-to-many or many-to-many relationship for query results. Example: `data class UserWithOrders(@Embedded val user: User, @Relation(parentColumn = "id", entityColumn = "userId") val orders: List<Order>)`. Room fetches users, then fetches all their orders, and stitches the results together — hiding SQL JOINs and result-set mapping. Always wrap `@Relation`-returning queries in `@Transaction` for consistency.

## Real-world usage

- **Offline-first apps** (Slack, Notion, Signal) — Room caches server data locally, UI reads from Room via Flow, background jobs sync to server via WorkManager.
- **Search history / bookmarks / drafts** — anything you don't want to lose across app restarts.
- **Paging 3 lists** — Room's `PagingSource` return type wires directly into Paging.
- **Reactive UI** — Room's Flow returns are what make Compose apps feel 'automatically alive'.
- **Multi-module apps** — one `:database` module owns Room; feature modules only see DAO interfaces.

## Advanced notes

- **`@TypeConverter`** — turn a `Date` into a `Long`, an `Enum` into a `String`, a `List<Int>` into a JSON string. Register a `@TypeConverters(...)` on the Database.
- **`@Index`** — add a database index on a column for fast lookups.
- **`@Fts4` / `@Fts3`** — full-text search entities.
- **`RawQuery`** — build queries dynamically at runtime (returns a `Cursor` or Kotlin type). Rarely needed.
- **`ROOM_ONLY_COMPILE`** — use `@DatabaseView` for pre-defined query 'views' as first-class types.
- **Prepopulating** — `Room.databaseBuilder(...).createFromAsset("prepopulated.db")` ships a canned DB.
- **Room + Compose** — `daoFlow.collectAsStateWithLifecycle()` in a `@Composable` — reactive persistence, zero boilerplate.
- **Kotlin Symbol Processing (KSP)** — Room 2.4+ supports KSP as a faster alternative to KAPT for its annotation processing; halve your build time by switching.
- **Room + Kotlin Multiplatform (KMP)** — Room went KMP-preview in 2024, sharing DBs between Android and iOS is now viable.

Any change to referenced tables auto-emits — UI stays in sync.
Atomic multi-step writes and consistent multi-query reads with @Relation.

Multi-module: `:database` module owns Room; feature modules only see DAO interfaces.
